messages.content: C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, Bacillus spp. są zdolne do produkcji biofilmu. - rephrase this
C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, and Bacillus spp. have the capacity for biofilm production.

C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, Bacillus spp. are capable of biofilm production. Forming a biofilm, they are resistant to drying, heating, anaerobic conditions, the presence of antibiotics and varying pH. Biofilm adhering to a variety of surfaces, such as tissues, catheters, enteral feeding tubes and work surfaces, poses a risk of cross-infection in the food and medical industries. - rephrase this

C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, and Bacillus spp. exhibit the ability to form biofilms, making them resistant to drying, heating, anaerobic environments, antibiotics, and changes in pH. These biofilms can adhere to different surfaces, including tissues, catheters, enteral feeding tubes, and work surfaces, potentially leading to cross-infection risks in both the food and medical sectors.

The aim of the research was to develop a prognostic model of biofilm formation by C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, Bacillus spp. on medical polyvinyl chloride, stainless steel and polypropylene, depending on temperature and number of microorganisms. In the model study, the intensity of biofilm formation was evaluated at 4°C on the surface of polypropylene and stainless steel, and at 37°C on the surface of medical polyvinyl chloride. The tested strains showed the ability to form biofilm at the tested temperatures. - rephrase this

The objective of the study was to create a predictive model for the formation of biofilms by C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, and Bacillus spp. on medical-grade polyvinyl chloride, stainless steel, and polypropylene surfaces, considering different temperatures and microbial concentrations. The research involved assessing the extent of biofilm formation at 4°C on polypropylene and stainless steel surfaces, as well as at 37°C on medical-grade polyvinyl chloride surfaces. The strains under investigation exhibited the capability of forming biofilms at the specific temperature conditions tested.
Based on the results obtained, nine response surface models of biofilm formation were developed. Most of the developed models are good fits, with high R2 and low MSE error values. - rephrase this

The obtained findings led to the development of nine response surface models for biofilm formation. The majority of these models demonstrated good accuracy, as evidenced by high R2 values and low MSE errors.

The obtained findings led to the development of nine response surface models for biofilm formation. They can be used in the assessment of microbiological risk in Breast Milk Banks, neonatal wards and neonatal intensive care units. - rephrase this

The findings have resulted in the creation of nine response surface models for biofilm formation. These models can be utilized to evaluate the microbiological risks in settings such as Breast Milk Banks, neonatal wards, and neonatal intensive care units.

1. biofilm is a complex structure composed of microorganisms It was first noticed by Antoni Van Leeuwenhoek on dental plaque in the 1770s. After the invention of electron microscopy, the biofilm was found to be composed of bacteria that are resistant to bactericides and bacteriostatic agents (Jamal et. al, 2015; Slavikn et al, 1997, Sedarat et al, 2019). - rephrase this

1. Biofilm is a intricate arrangement consisting of microorganisms. Its existence was initially observed by Antoni Van Leeuwenhoek on dental plaque during the 1770s. With the advent of electron microscopy, it was discovered that biofilms are predominantly composed of bacteria that possess resistance against bactericides and bacteriostatic agents (Jamal et al., 2015; Slavikn et al., 1997; Sedarat et al., 2019).

Biofilms increase the incidence of catheter-related infections. It is believed that 80% of all human infections are associated with biofilms, and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has highlighted that more than 65% of all nosocomial infections can be attributed to biofilms (Percival et al., 2017). Biofilm-forming microorganisms are resistant to desiccation, heating, anaerobic conditions, the presence of antibiotics and varying pH. - rephrase this

The presence of biofilms contributes to a higher occurrence of catheter-related infections. It is estimated that biofilms are associated with approximately 80% of all human infections, with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) identifying that over 65% of all hospital-acquired infections can be attributed to biofilms (Percival et al., 2017). Microorganisms capable of forming biofilms exhibit resistance to drying, heating, anaerobic conditions, the presence of antibiotics, and changes in pH.

There are four phases in biofilm formation. The first phase is reversible adhesion – bacteria, under the influence of Brownian Movements, gravitational forces and van der Waals forces, are transferred to the surface and cover the colonized surface. In the second phase (irreversible adhesion), bacteria increase the synthesis of EPS, in the next stage, microorganisms multiply and differentiate as a result of activation or inhibition of gene expression (third stage: biofilm maturation). There are many microenvironments within a mature biofilm that vary in terms of oxygen concentration, pH, nutrient availability and cell density. Ostatnia faza to dyspersja biofilmu, czyli oderwanie komórek od zasiedlanej powierzchni oraz ich rozproszenie w środowisku.  - rephrase this

Biofilm formation occurs in four distinct phases. The initial phase is reversible adhesion, where bacteria are transferred and adhere to the surface due to Brownian movements, gravitational forces, and van der Waals forces. In the subsequent irreversible adhesion phase, bacteria enhance the synthesis of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS). This is followed by the third stage, known as biofilm maturation, during which microorganisms proliferate and undergo differentiation triggered by gene expression modulation. Within a mature biofilm, there exist various microenvironments with varying oxygen levels, pH, nutrient availability, and cell density. The final phase, known as biofilm dispersion, entails the detachment of cells from the colonized surface and their scattering within the surrounding environment.

It is extremely important to know the influence of various environmental factors on the development of biofilm. Gathering detailed information on the dynamics of biofilm formation enables a mathematical predictive model. The model describes the response of microorganisms to specific environmental conditions and can be used to predict the growth, survival and inactivation of microorganisms. - rephrase this

Understanding the impact of different environmental factors on biofilm development is highly crucial. Detailed knowledge about the dynamics of biofilm formation allows for the creation of a mathematical predictive model. This model elucidates how microorganisms respond to specific environmental conditions and can be utilized to forecast the growth, survival, and inactivation of these microorganisms.

Classical methods of microbiological analysis performed on a large scale are associated with significant costs and time necessary to obtain results enabling the estimation of microbiological risk (Rosiak and Kołożyn-Krajewska, 2003; Lasik et al., 2016; Rosiak et al., 2018). There are several mathematical studies of predictive models of biofilm formation in the scientific literature. The cardinal model proposed by Rosso in 1995 (Rosso and Robinson, 2001), defining the cardinal values (minimum, optimum, maximum) of structurally correlated environmental factors, was used to describe biofilm formation by Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica ser. - rephrase this

Conventional microbiological analysis methods, when performed on a large scale, often entail significant costs and require a considerable amount of time to acquire results that allow for the estimation of microbiological risk (Rosiak and Kołożyn-Krajewska, 2003; Lasik et al., 2016; Rosiak et al., 2018). Several mathematical studies have been conducted to develop predictive models for biofilm formation in the scientific literature. A notable model proposed by Rosso in 1995 (Rosso and Robinson, 2001) utilizes cardinal values (minimum, optimum, maximum) of structurally related environmental factors to describe biofilm formation by Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica ser.

C. sakazakii bacteria have been isolated from human milk and surfaces that come into contact with milk (e.g. sink, baby bottles, breast pumps). C. sakazakii is responsible for severe neonatal infections and complications. They occur as a result of feeding breast milk or milk replacer, and bacteria enter the milk as a result of improper handling of milk, e.g. improper washing and disinfection of bottles and breast pumps (Haston et al., 2023; Lachowska et al., 2021; Zeng et al. al., 2018; Sundararajan et al., 2018; McMullan et al., 2018; Jackson et al., 2015; Chaves et al., 2018; Bowen et al., 2017). - rephrase this

C. sakazakii bacteria have been found in human milk as well as on surfaces that come into contact with milk, such as sinks, baby bottles, and breast pumps. The presence of C. sakazakii poses a significant risk of severe neonatal infections and complications. These infections can occur when infants consume contaminated breast milk or milk replacer, with bacteria entering the milk due to improper handling practices such as inadequate washing and disinfection of bottles and breast pumps (Haston et al., 2023; Lachowska et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2018; Sundararajan et al., 2018; McMullan et al., 2018; Jackson et al., 2015; Chaves et al., 2018; Bowen et al., 2017).

With the real risk of contamination of breast milk by C. sakazakii, E. cloacae and Bacillus spp. and the risk of these bacteria forming a biofilm on milk-contact surfaces, the challenge is to assess the risk of biofilm formation on working surfaces in institutions where premature and newborn babies are fed.

Given the genuine concern regarding the potential contamination of breast milk by C. sakazakii, E. cloacae, and Bacillus spp., as well as the risk of biofilm development on surfaces in contact with milk, there is a pressing need to evaluate the likelihood of biofilm formation on work surfaces in institutions that provide feeding care for premature and newborn infants.

Risk assessment components include hazard characterization and assessment, which uses mathematical predictive models, among others. Risk assessment components include hazard characterization and assessment, which uses mathematical predictive models, among others. The surfaces selected for testing are surfaces that come into contact with food and human milk.

Risk assessment incorporates various components, including hazard characterization and assessment. Mathematical predictive models are one of the tools used in hazard characterization and assessment. In the context of assessing the risk of biofilm formation, surfaces that come into contact with food and human milk are typically selected for testing and evaluation.

The aim of the study was to develop a predictive model of biofilm formation by bacteria on three work surfaces, depending on temperature and number of microorganisms. The surfaces selected for testing are surfaces that come into contact with food and human milk. Enteral feeding tubes are made of polyvinyl chloride. Polypropylene is used in the production of bottles for storing and feeding babies, stainless steel is an example of a low-adhesive surface widely used in food production. - rephrase this

The objective of the study was to create a predictive model for biofilm formation by bacteria on three specific work surfaces, taking into account variations in temperature and microbial load. The chosen surfaces for testing are those that have direct contact with food and human milk. For instance, enteral feeding tubes are typically composed of polyvinyl chloride, while polypropylene is commonly employed in the manufacturing of bottles for storing and feeding infants. Stainless steel, on the other hand, represents a low-adhesive surface that is widely utilized in the food production industry.

2. The research material consisted of three strains of bacteria ( Cronobacter sakazakii ATCC 29544, Enterobacter cloacae , Bacillus spp.), obtained from the own collection of the Department of Hygiene and Food Quality Management, Institute of Human Nutrition Sciences of the Warsaw University of Life Sciences, Warsaw University of Life Sciences. Three different surfaces were used to assess biofilm production: medical polyvinyl chloride (PVC) (Sumi, Poland), stainless steel (AISI 304), polypropylene (NUK MedicPro). Depending on the material, different incubation times and temperatures were used (Table 1): 37°C, 25°C, 12°C and 4°C - rephrase this

The research materials in this study consisted of three bacterial strains (Cronobacter sakazakii ATCC 29544, Enterobacter cloacae, and Bacillus spp.) obtained from the collection of the Department of Hygiene and Food Quality Management, Institute of Human Nutrition Sciences, Warsaw University of Life Sciences. To evaluate biofilm production, three different surfaces were utilized: medical-grade polyvinyl chloride (PVC) from Sumi (Poland), stainless steel (AISI 304), and polypropylene (NUK MedicPro). Incubation times and temperatures varied depending on the material used, as outlined in Table 1, encompassing conditions of 37°C, 25°C, 12°C, and 4°C.
Then, 150 μL was taken into a new sterile 96-well polystyrene plate and the absorbance at 600 nm was determined with a microplate reader (SoftMax Pro, USA). - rephrase this

Next, a volume of 150 μL was extracted and transferred into a fresh sterile 96-well polystyrene plate, followed by the measurement of absorbance at 600 nm utilizing a microplate reader (SoftMax Pro, USA).

The cutoff optical density (ODc) for the plate assay was defined as three standard deviations above the mean OD of the negative control, and the strains were classified as follows: Non-adherent (-) OD600 < Ep; weak (+) Length < OD600 < 2 x Length; moderate (++) 2 x Ep < OD600 < 4 x Ep; strong (+++) 4 x Ep < OD600. - rephrase this

The cutoff optical density (ODc) for the plate assay was determined as three times the standard deviation above the average OD of the negative control. Using this criterion, the strains were classified into different categories as follows: Non-adherent (-) when OD600 < Ep; weakly adherent (+) when Length < OD600 < 2 x Length; moderately adherent (++) when 2 x Ep < OD600 < 4 x Ep; and strongly adherent (+++) when OD600 > 4 x Ep.
The test was repeated in triplicate, in two separate experiments for each strain, and the results were averaged. - rephrase this
The test was carried out in triplicate, spanning two independent experiments for each strain, and the obtained results were averaged for analysis.

The parameters of the response surface model were estimated using iterative procedures using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The number of iterations for any model did not exceed 50. Calculations were made in TableCurve 3D, v.4.0.05 for Windows, SYSTAT Software Inc., using non-linear functions from the Gaussian, Extreme Value, Log Normal and Logistic Peak families (Tab. 2) - rephrase this

The response surface model parameters were estimated through iterative procedures utilizing the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The maximum number of iterations for any given model did not exceed 50. The calculations were performed using TableCurve 3D, v.4.0.05 for Windows, a software developed by SYSTAT Software Inc. Non-linear functions from various families, including Gaussian, Extreme Value, Log Normal, and Logistic Peak, were utilized (as shown in Table 2) during the analysis.

The R 2 regression coefficient and the DF Adjusted R 2 regression coefficient verified by the number of degrees of freedom were used to explain the variability of the analyzed variables. - rephrase this
The R2 regression coefficient and the adjusted R2 regression coefficient, validated by the degrees of freedom, were employed to elucidate the extent of variability in the analyzed variables.

The correctness of the fit of the developed models was statistically assessed by determining the smallest value of the sum of squares of the error between the empirical and theoretical values - the mean squared error (MSE). A graphical 3D evaluation of the developed models was also performed by comparing the observed and predicted OD-values. - rephrase this

The accuracy of the developed models was assessed statistically by evaluating the mean squared error (MSE), which involved determining the minimum value of the sum of squared errors between the empirical and theoretical values. Additionally, a graphical 3D analysis was conducted, comparing the observed and predicted OD-values, to evaluate the performance of the developed models.

3. Temperature was selected to analyze the strength of biofilm formation on the surface of polyvinyl chloride (enteral tube). 37 °C as the temperature inside the human body. In the case of stainless steel and polypropylene surfaces, biofilm formation was studied at a cooling temperature of 4 °C. At the same time, the growth of the tested bacteria was assessed in the tested range of time and temperature. - rephrase this

Temperature was chosen as a variable to investigate the intensity of biofilm formation on polyvinyl chloride (enteral tube) surfaces, with a temperature of 37 °C representing the conditions found inside the human body. On the other hand, the formation of biofilms on stainless steel and polypropylene surfaces was examined at a lower temperature of 4 °C. Simultaneously, the growth of the tested bacteria was evaluated within the range of time and temperature under investigation.

At 4 °C, the bacteria did not grow in the time range tested at any concentration of inoculum. At 37 °C, the bacteria showed growth from 9 h of incubation with concentrations of S, M, L. With the exception of C. sakazakii strain ATCC 29544, which at the lowest concentration of inoculum grew from 12 h of incubation. - rephrase this
